fragments.content: Clinical trial failure rates represent the proportion of investigational drugs that do not advance from one development phase to the next or fail to gain regulatory approval. Overall, only about 10-15% of drugs entering Phase I ultimately receive FDA approval. Failure rates vary significantly by therapeutic area: oncology has the highest attrition (~90% fail in Phase II/III), while vaccines and hematology have lower rates. The likelihood of success (LOS) from Phase I to approval averages 7.9% across all indications. Most failures occur in Phase II due to lack of efficacy (40-50% of failures) or safety concerns (20-30%). Understanding failure patterns informs go/no-go decisions and portfolio management.

Phase transition success rates (approximate, varies by study and era): Phase I to Phase II: 60-70% (safety/PK issues cause most failures); Phase II to Phase III: 30-40% (efficacy signal insufficient or safety concerns emerge); Phase III to approval: 50-60% (failure to confirm efficacy, safety signals in larger populations, or commercial considerations). Oncology has particularly low Phase II-III success (~25-30%) due to reliance on surrogate endpoints that don't always predict survival benefit. CNS/psychiatry also has high failure rates (~80-90% in Phase II/III) due to poor translational models and high placebo responses. Strategies to improve success include: better target validation, biomarker-driven patient selection, adaptive designs, earlier go/no-go decisions, and learning from failed programs.

Analysis of failure causes: Lack of efficacy accounts for 40-50% of Phase II/III failures—often due to poor target validation, inadequate preclinical models, wrong patient population, or insufficient drug exposure. Safety/toxicity causes 20-30% of failures—sometimes predictable from mechanism (on-target toxicity) or idiosyncratic (off-target). Strategic/commercial reasons (changing priorities, market dynamics, funding) account for 10-15%. Poor pharmacokinetics, formulation issues, or manufacturing problems cause 5-10%. Time trends show modest improvement in success rates over past decade due to better patient selection (biomarkers, precision medicine), improved trial design, and more rigorous go/no-go criteria. First-in-class drugs have lower success rates than follow-on products. External factors like regulatory stringency and competitive landscape also influence continuation decisions.
